data.title: Bitcoin: Blood On The Streets, Buy Signals Flashing
data.description:
To say the very least, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has had a tumultuous two months. It topped in early October at about $126,000 and is now down at $82,000 at the time of writing. I must admit that I was caught rather flat-footed by this move, because I had expected a run-up to over $175,000 and an extended cycle that would turn somewhere in Q1 2026. This would be aligned with the 4-year cycle, albeit slightly extended in time frame. What seems to have happened is the 4-year cycle has still held up, but the top arrived earlier than expected.

In this missive, I want to contextualize where we are in regard to a few very consistent indicators that are good at predicting an upturn in BTC. I will highlight a handful of indicators, discuss what they are, and discuss what they are saying about the market.

Options/Volatility Surface Indicators

The main thing to grasp is that options markets tell us much of what we need to know with regard to times of extreme fear. I haven’t found them to be a great indication of exuberance, but fear is where it gets it right. This is likely due to people using options in certain discernible actions when instances of extreme fear arise. These behaviors show up in the implied volatility surface and can be spotted when compared against the state of the surface at other times.

The first options-based indicator is, of course, the VIX – a model free implied volatility measure, called model free because it does not use the Black-Scholes options pricing model to compute an implied volatility. VIX is computed using a strip of vanilla options. Like the SPX, BTC has a VIX indicator too. DVOL is the name of this index, and it is computed using Deribit options on BTC. Here is what DVOL looks like:

You can see that it has reached the levels in early April 2025 where BTC bottomed from the tariffs scare. However, it is nowhere near the peaks from Terra Luna in May 2022 nor from FTX in November 2022. Does it have to get there to find a bottom?

Now, another important fact to know about Bitcoin is that, as opposed to equities, it tends to exhibit positive spot-vol covariance, which means that as the spot price moves up, volatility can actually increase. This limits the usefulness of simple VIX as a singular indicator for fear.

The next one is skew: normally regarded as the difference in the implied volatilities of the 25 delta call and the 25 delta put. When puts are priced at a significant premium to calls (assuming both are the same delta), it indicates significantly more demand for protection. Here is the skew of BTC options, with many different maturities.

Skew has been trending up for months. The 3 month skew is actually the highest it's been since the Silicon Valley Bank incident in March 2023. The 1-month skew is where it was in April 2025. However, skew on all timeframes is far off from the FTX incident in November 2022, which marked the bottom of the last cycle.

Next up is the term structure of implied volatility. This is the difference in IV between the 1-month expiry and the 6-month expiry. In general, backwardation in the IV term structure, where near-term is higher than further term, indicates a high degree of fear. This is consistent in equity markets as well. You can see it in both the term structure of VIX futures (which measures the future price of the SPX 1-month IV) and in the term structure of SPX options at each tenor (which is the construct we have here).

The term structure is now in deep backwardation and quite close to the FTX bottom. It is the highest it's been all cycle. What is also remarkable is how smoothly it climbed since September as the BTC moved to a new all-time high and then crashed over 30% in the course of two months.

MRVR On-Chain Indicator

MRVR uses the concept of realized gains of coins based on the last time they moved on the blockchain. The idea is that cycle bottoms form when the aggregated coins across the entire supply are in the red. The consistency of this indicator is quite remarkable. As with options indicators, it’s not great at calling tops but it has a knack for finding the cycle bottoms.

Considering that MRVR is now at 1.47, it means that the supply right now is still up 47%.

At this point, there are a few things to note. The start of 2024 was also the start of the institutional era in Bitcoin. While it is true that institutions will ultimately do on-chain transactions at some point, it is also true that the way institutions do things means that a single UTXO does not have to move for an institutional transaction to happen. What I mean by this is that when BTC was a person-to-person network, a transaction was almost always on-chain. When institutions are dealing with BTC that are held by custodians, the on-chain movement of a UTXO is often unnecessary because the same custodian can hold the UTXO but just update their ledger with the new ownership. This is a fundamental change in the market structure of how BTC is used, and this change will likely affect where MRVR bottoms out.

Second, just because the aggregate supply of BTC is up 47% does not mean the market must fall by that much before finding a bottom. A simple way to see this is to consider if all the UTXOs suddenly moved. This would automatically reset the cost basis of the entire supply to the current market price, since MRVR starts counting a coin as “new” right after it moves. If this were to happen, then no sales of BTC would need to happen (thus price cannot change), but MRVR would immediately go to 1 because the current supply would have a cost basis equal to the current market price.

Third, it is very likely the market finds a floor well before MRVR falls below 1. Considering that the pattern has persisted for four cycles, it is likely that traders will front-run the event and cause the bottom to form much earlier than normal. Ever since the launch of the spot ETFs, MRVR has found support around 1.7–1.9. And unlike prior cycles, the volatility of MRVR has been much more muted, along with the volatility of BTC.

Verdict

I’m assigning a Strong Buy rating to BTC based on the market internals due to the risk-reward presented by the market today. I believe it is very important to understand that the systemic-level risks from FTX, Celsius, Voyager, and 3AC in the last cycle are simply not present this cycle. There isn’t the same kind of opaque leverage in the system. In truth, the only bad news around BTC has been that long term holders have been selling, but even this should be expected if you consider that half of all BTC that will ever exist was released in the first four years and could have been mined using personal computers at effectively zero cost basis. Those early adopters are at the stage of rotating out of BTC and into enjoying life.

The 4-year cycle will likely break soon. It arguably already has, given that some things happened earlier than expected. For example, the price hit a new ATH before the Halving in April 2024. It also entered into months of consolidation after the Halving, which was very unusual. Traditionally the bear markets that end the cycle have lasted a year with over 70% drawdowns, BTC is now much more mature with an institutional audience that, while not prone to panicking, is prone to rebalancing. This kind of behavior is very likely to create a floor before a more dramatic downturn.

The ultimate backdrop of all of this is a U.S. government that is entering midterms with a record amount of issues, which can only be solved by injecting liquidity into the system. After Powell exits, the new Fed Chair will likely be much more accommodative to President Trump, who has been very adamant about lower interest rates and sending markets, including crypto, much higher.

Risk

The main risk to this thesis is that it plays out exactly like the traditional 4-year cycle. This could have us see a bear market that extends to the summer of 2026. BTC is already down 30%. Assuming a 60% drawdown from top to bottom would put the bottom at ~$50,000. That is a 40% drop from current levels.

As I’ve said before, I don’t see any real fundamental issues that could hurt BTC. The macro and the market internals all signal that a reversal is near. The only real risk, therefore—in my opinion—would be the market getting ahead of the 4-year cycle and turning it into a self-fulfilling prophecy.

Conclusion

While I was correct that the bear market in BTC would happen within 9 months of my last article, I was wrong in that I expected a larger rally with a blow-off top. What has transpired instead is a quick -30% crash. In the long term, I view this as a favorable turn of events that distributes coins to much stronger hands, while flushing out much of the fragile derivatives leverage.

Right now BTC looks very attractive based on the short-term indicators of fear, with the main risk being that the 4-year cycle continues.

data.title: Brookfield Asset Management: Reality Check For High Growth Rates
data.description:
In July 2025, I published my last article about the Canadian alternative asset manager Brookfield Asset Management Ltd. (BAM) and I argued that caution was the better choice at this point. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

I certainly won’t advise anyone to short Brookfield Asset Management, although I see another steep drop (30% to 50%) being very likely. We are dealing with a great business, but one trading for high valuation multiples and the risk of contracting valuation multiples should not be ignored. In combination with growth estimates by analysts and management maybe being too optimistic for the years to come, I would not buy the stock close to all-time highs. In my opinion, we will get the chance to buy the stock at least for its April 2025 lows again and probably even lower.

Up until now, we have not reached April 2025, but the stock declined 11% meanwhile and is currently trading 22% below its previous all-time high. Especially in the last few days, the sell-off has accelerated, and Brookfield Asset Management has lost more than 10% in value since last Wednesday.

In the following article I will argue once again that Brookfield Asset Management is rather a “hold” than a “buy”—despite the stock price being lower. I still ascribe a rather high probability to the scenario of lower growth rates in the years to come due to a potential recession and the AI spending boom coming to an end. And Brookfield Asset Management will be affected by this—despite reporting high growth rates and solid quarterly results up until now.

Quarterly Results

On November 7, 2025, Brookfield Asset Management reported third-quarter results for fiscal 2025. And while the company did beat slightly on earnings per share estimates, it missed revenue estimates by $90 billion (about 7% below expectations). Year-over-year fee revenues increased from $1,210 million in Q3 '24 to $1,390 million in Q3 '25 – resulting in 14.9% top-line growth. Fee-related earnings also increased 17.1% year-over-year from $644 million in Q3 '24 to $754 million in Q3 '25, while distributable earnings per share increased only 7.9% year-over-year from $0.38 in Q3 '24 to $0.41 in Q3 '25.

During the third quarter, Brookfield Asset Management deployed $23 billion in capital—a record quarter in deployment. The money was especially deployed in infrastructure ($9.3 billion during the quarter) and in credit ($9.9 billion). In the case of infrastructure, the money was put towards the acquisition of Hotwire Communication ($3.8 billion) and the acquisition of Colonial Enterprises ($3.4 billion).

While Brookfield Asset Management deployed $23 billion in capital, it raised about $30 billion during the third quarter (another record). Money was especially raised for Renewable Power & Transition ($6.3 billion) and Credit ($16.0 billion).

Year-over-year, fee-bearing capital increased 7.8% year-over-year from $539 billion in Q3 '24 to $581 billion in Q3 '25. This was especially due to inflows of $92 billion (partly offset by $22 billion in outflows and $26 billion in distributions). Credit still has the most fee-bearing capital—$263 - $263 billion and contributed 7.8% year-over-year growth. The two other drivers of growth were Real Estate (8.5% year-over-year growth) as well as Renewable Power & Transition (19.0% year-over-year growth).

Growth Rates

Analysts as well as management are remaining very optimistic that Brookfield Asset Management will continue to grow at a high pace. For the years between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2029, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 15.34% in these five years.

Management is similar or even more optimistic. During the last Investor Day in September 2025, the company looked back at the goal set for the last five years to double between 2020 and 2025 and the goal for the next five years until 2030 is to double again. Fee-bearing capital is expected to grow from $563 billion in September 2025 to $1.2 trillion in 2030 resulting in a CAGR of at least 16%.

During the last earnings call, CFO Hadley Peer Marshall commented:

These drivers should enable us to double our business over the next 5 years with fee-related earnings reaching $5.8 billion, distributable earnings reaching $5.9 billion, and fee-bearing capital reaching $1.2 trillion.

Management is seeing different drivers of growth for the years to come. Especially Credit is expected to drive growth, and fee-bearing assets are expected to increase from $254 billion in 2025 to $640 billion in 2030. The company will combine different strategies to reach these growth targets in the next five years—like the acquisition of the remaining 26% stake of Oaktree and Wealth Solutions growing at a high pace.

CEO Bruce Flatt commented on the path for the next five years:

Another important milestone was our recently announced agreement to acquire the remaining 26% in Oaktree Capital Management. As you know, one of the most respected names in global credit investing. When we partnered with Oaktree 6 years ago, the goal was to combine our global scale and real asset expertise with Oaktree's deep credit experience and value-oriented culture. That partnership exceeded expectations, enabling the rapid expansion of our credit platform, supporting the launch of Brookfield Wealth Solutions, and driving a 75% increase in Oaktree's asset base. Bringing Oaktree fully into Brookfield is the next natural step. It combines the scale and reach of our nearly $350 billion credit platform, enables deeper collaboration across our businesses from origination and underwriting to distribution and analytics. Most importantly, it enhances our ability to deliver the full breadth of Brookfield's credit capabilities to clients.

And finally, Brookfield Asset Management currently has $125 billion in uncalled commitments at this point, with $55 billion in funds not being fee-earning. This could lead to $550 million in additional fee revenue for the company—even without additional fundraising.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

So far, Brookfield Asset Management is still presenting itself as a business with high-growth potential and stable earnings. However, the stock is also trading for high valuation multiples. At the time of writing, BAM is trading for 30.4 times earnings, and although this is very close to the lowest P/E ratio Brookfield Asset Management was trading for in almost three years, a valuation multiple of 30 can only be justified by high-growth rates.

When looking at the price-free-cash-flow ratio, we see even higher multiples, as Brookfield Asset Management is currently trading for a P/FCF ratio of 38.6.

Aside from simple valuation multiples, we can also use a discounted cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock (or calculate what growth rates are necessary for the stock to be fairly valued). As always, we use a discount rate of 10% (as this is the annual return we like to achieve at least) and calculate with the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (which was 1,631 million). The basis for our calculation will be the distributable earnings of the last twelve months, which were $2,577 million.

If we are optimistic and assume Brookfield Asset Management can grow 15% annually for the next ten years (analysts only presented a growth target for the next five years, but I think management is optimistic high-growth rates will continue), followed by 6% growth until perpetuity, we get an intrinsic value of $74.48 for the stock, and Brookfield Asset Management is clearly undervalued.

In my opinion, this is too optimistic, and I would not bet on Brookfield Asset Management growing 15% annually. In a more pessimistic scenario, we assume “only” 10% growth for the next 10 years (which is still a high-growth rate) and get an intrinsic value of $52.43 making the stock only slightly undervalued at this point. There is also a reason for me being more skeptical than management and analysts—the AI boom and focus on credit.

How Predictable Are Mid-Teen Growth Rates?

This is probably the question it comes down to: How predictable are growth rates around 15% for the business, and how likely is it that Brookfield Asset Management will be able to keep up these high growth rates? And management is not only describing 87% of fee revenue as long-term or permanent (and therefore very predictable), it is assuming the share will grow to 92% in 2030.

And fees for Brookfield Asset Management might be very predictable, and the company can assume it will earn similar fees in 2026 as in 2025. However, Brookfield Asset Management needs high, double-digit growth to justify the current share price, and these growth rates are less predictable and stable in my opinion.

Problem: AI Boom

Management is very optimistic about artificial intelligence and how it could contribute to growth in the years to come:

The acceleration of AI and data digital infrastructure, the accelerating demand for electricity and the improving strength in the real estate markets, each of these themes plays directly to our strength as an owner, operator and investor in real assets and together, they are fueling multiyear growth across the business.

Management is also expecting the AI-related infrastructure market to grow at a high pace over the next decade:

We estimate that AI-related infrastructure investments will exceed $7 trillion over the next decade. Brookfield's unique position, owning and operating across the full energy and digital infrastructure value chain gives us a tremendous advantage in capturing this opportunity.

Although Brookfield Asset Management is only collecting fees and not affected as much by a bubble bursting, it is always a problem if spending for AI and infrastructure will slow down. Brookfield Asset Management might not be affected by huge losses or face the risk of bankruptcy (which will probably happen to some AI companies), but it will be affected by growth rates for its fees slowing down to a point where investors might not spend additional money on AI for several quarters or years, putting Brookfield Asset Management’s fundraising under pressure. And the business must grow at a high pace to be fairly valued.

Problem: Credit

Currently, the credit business is extremely lucrative and a driver of growth for Brookfield Asset Management. But, in case of an economic slowdown, the credit business is facing huge risks, in my opinion. Banks, asset management, credit, and lending—these are categories associated with high levels of risk in case of a recession or economic slowdown.

Of course, Brookfield Asset Management is facing limited risks as it doesn’t lend the money itself and is therefore collecting fees while third parties are the lenders and are therefore facing the highest credit and default risks. It is not enough for Brookfield Asset Management to keep its fee-bearing capital (and therefore fees) stable or grow in the single digits. The company must grow in the double digits to justify the stock price, and in case of a recession, I don’t see the company growing its fee at a high pace.

Risks Summarized

Not only is the current AI boom probably a bubble that might burst and lead to much lower growth rates in the next few quarters (or years), but credit as well as real estate might also see much lower growth rates in case of a recession. And as Brookfield Asset Management is depending on high-growth rates to justify the current stock price, a recession or economic slowdown can create a problem for the stock price and shareholders.

Conclusion

I still see the risk of declining stock prices in the coming months and quarters. And while I don’t have a crystal ball either (similar to every other investor), I think the stock will decline at least to about $40 again. If it will decline further seems very difficult to predict. On the one hand, we have a business growing at a high pace (around 15%) and strong hints that high growth might continue (so far it is raising capital and still has “dry powder,” and a recession – if it happens – might also be an opportunity to deploy capital again and find great investments).

On the other hand, a recession and slowdown in spending – especially in credit and AI—might pose a high risk for Brookfield Asset Management. Sentiment might also turn in such a scenario and lead to a declining stock price and the risk of a steep sell-off. And as investors, I would rather stay on the sidelines at this point.

data.title: TE Connectivity: Reset Is Real, But The Valuation Already Knows It
data.description:
Investment Thesis

TE Connectivity's (TEL) Q4 '25 results confirmed virtually all the points that I had made in my previous thesis. The company is more diversified, stable, and being driven by secular demand (rather than by cyclical volumes) in increasing measure. The DDN segment continues to grow at a pace far exceeding expectations and is clearly and most durably representative of TEL's relevance in the area of accelerated compute. Energy is evolving into a multi-year utility infrastructure platform as opposed to being a tactical segment. Although softness in the sensor space continues to weigh on the transportation segment, this segment has proved to have a degree of stability in recent periods. The company's margins have stabilized above 20%; the company's free cash flow generation has been strong; however, the company's valuation, which in my opinion broadly factored in all the transformation and improvement. Therefore, I am reaffirming my Hold rating.

Topline Analysis & Outlook

TEL had $17.3bn revenues for FY25 (+9% on a reported basis and +6% on an organic basis). Q4'25 revenue grew to $4.75bn with (+17%/+11% (YoY)) on a reported and organic basis. Importantly, orders in Q4'25 represented a 22% (YoY) and 5% (QoQ) increase and provide a solid visibility for the company to generate another year of solid top-line growth in FY26. Management is guiding revenue in Q1'26 to ~$4.5bn (i.e., low double-digit organic growth).

The aspect of the portfolio that is most profitably transformative is Digital Data & Networking. The timelines looking at FY25 have demonstrated that the growth in TEL's accelerated compute business is not only real but accelerating faster than our most optimistic views. AI interconnect revenue, which included accelerated compute, approached nearly $900mn in FY25, and I expect that to accelerate in FY26 as the new GPU platforms propagate more widely into deployment and the customer base expands from just hyperscale to include cloud enterprise and edge workloads. The change here is architectural; the GPU racks are getting denser, hotter, and power hungry, and TE is providing the necessary high-speed copper, power distribution, and custom backplane solutions to follow along. These sockets are typically "sticky," fueled by long qualification cycles and high switching costs. I expect DDN will be the fastest growing segment of the company through FY26, with higher growth rates at the beginning, but tapering into the mid-teens or lower twenties range by FY27-FY28 as the installed base continues to grow with the increasing reliance on AI and accelerated compute workloads.

Energy delivered yet another strong quarter, with revenues increasing 83% and 24% on an organic basis, and continues to evidence what appears to be a multi-year growth curve based on grid hardening, undergrounding mandates, renewable interconnections, and the expanded portfolio resulting from the Richards acquisition. Unlike many previous cycles, much of the investment that is being made currently on the grids in North America and Europe is regulatory-based, multi-year in nature, and linked to reliability requirements. With Richards, TE is now involved in a much more comprehensive part of the medium voltage and distribution value chain. I expect Energy to show double-digit growth in FY26 and then settle into a high single-digit to low teens growth profile through FY28 as comparables normalize, but demand is structurally enhanced.

Automation & Connected Living also showed some voluntary re-normalization in Q4 with organic growth of 11%, telling us that we have cycled through much of the inventory digestion phase that challenged the business for much of FY25. Asia appears to be stabilizing, and the order flow looks to point to a healthier industrial backdrop into FY26. I expect ACL to continue to deliver sustained high single digits of growth in FY26 and begin to moderate gradually to mid-single digits of growth into FY27/FY28 as industrial PMIs stabilize and capex patterns return to more normal rhythms.

Aerospace, Defense & Marine: Achieved steady performance with 7% organic growth in the fourth quarter of fiscal year 25. Strength in the production of narrow-body aircraft and steady performance in defense activity contributed to the result. Although Aerospace, Defense & Marine is not a material growth driver, stability is the one segment that provides a predictable program, stability. I think AD&M will continue to have mid single-digit growth profiles for the next couple of years with ongoing fleet replacement cycles and strong Western defense budgets.

Transportation segment Q4 FY25 revenue was $2.41bn, +4% & +2% (organic). The geographical divide doesn't appear to be changing: Asia continues to balance the persistent softness in Europe and North America. An increase in the content of electrification, ADAS, and zonal architectures replaces a flattening of the global unit volumes, which signals a stabilization. Although the recovery is uneven, commercial transportation picked up a little when fleet replacement cycles began to pick up in some places. Sensors remain a headwind. I expect Transportation to have a low- to mid single-digit growth profile through FY28, and increasingly take on the role of the stabilizing base of the portfolio as opposed to the growth engine.

As a whole, TE is entering FY26 with a more balanced revenue mix and structurally stronger demand profile than at any time during the last decade. DDN and Energy represent the secular growth engines, ACL is returning to normalized levels of absorption, AD&M is showing program-driven stabilization, and Transportation is providing duration.

Margin Outlook

TE closed FY25 with a consolidated adjusted operating margin of 19.7% (+80bps YoY), and 19.9% (+130bps YoY) for Q4. To me, it is not just the number that is important, but more the consistency: TEL maintained a ~20% margin profile under very different end market conditions, and it did it without taking advantage of temporary cost actions. Mix-up, volume leverage, in the right places, disciplined execution across the portfolio came from the uplift. Management's Q1'26 guide, with the new non-GAAP treatment that excludes intangible amortization, indicates a stable starting point; however, even considering the company on a like-for-like basis, the exit rate going into FY26 is an indication of a structurally stronger operating model than the company carried heading into FY25.

Transportation Solutions had a Q4 adjusted operating margin of 19.4%, essentially flat year on year, and certainly closed FY25 at close to the ~20% band management had long indicated as the steady state level for this business. The pattern throughout the year was familiar: content growth was offsetting uneven regional production, pricing held up, and sensors were one recurring drag on blended profitability. The segment is not set up for major margin expansion from here, based on global auto build being expected to stay broadly flat, but what stands out is the stability. TE no longer needs volume growth to defend Transportation margins - electronics content across EV platforms, zonal architectures, and ADAS systems is now the burger. Through FY26-FY28, and looking right now, I view Transportation as one of these margin anchors, one that's stable, predictable, and guaranteed support and not unit support.

Industrial Solutions is the place where the structural shifts are more visible. The segment had a Q4 adjusted operating margin of 20.3% (+300 bps YoY), and the company's management has stated that the second half of FY25 was running at ~20% (full year FY margin at 19.1%). The broader argument is that Industrial is now trading at a consistent margin level that was simply unattainable just a few years ago. Most of the drivers are a product mix. Digital Data & Networking is the most accretive contributor, with AI interconnect programs scaling into higher contribution architectures with long qualification cycles and engineering-heavy solutions. Energy is increasingly coming into play as a durable contributor to margin rather than an opportunistic contributor; the early stages of the Richards integration cause the typical friction in terms of footprints, alignments, but the underpinning economics of the undergrounding, modernizing the grid, and grid renewable interconnection economics are margin supportive as volumes build. Automation & Connected Living is slowly recovering back to normal incremental absorption as the correction of the manufacturers' inventory fades away, and Aerospace & Defense & Marine continues offering the normal program economics to refine and smooth the consolidated industrial profile. The common theme is that the mix at Industrial is continuing to move towards businesses with a better underlying contribution structure - i.e., a more secure underlying margin base.

Stepping back, TE comes into FY26 with a margin foundation that is more bothersome to navigate and structurally supported than they've seen in any recent cycle. Transportation is a floor that we can count on, Industrial is the Mix that is driven Upside, and the portfolio is now driven to the segments that compound the earnings rather than oscillate with short-cycle Volatility. There will be quarter-to-quarter fluctuations related to the phasing of AI programs or utility projects, but the medium-term outlook is simple:

Financial

Peer Comparison And Valuation

TEL's current trading multiples are a broad reflection of structural improvements in its portfolio. On forward numbers, the stock is trading around FWD P/E of 21~22x & 14.8x of FWD EV/ EBITDA, which puts the company in the low end of the highest growth connectivity companies but above the companies that have more cyclicality exposure in industrial components. The overall peer group is clustering at ~23x on FWD P/E, and ~17x on EV/ EBITDA, implying that TEL is neither undervalued nor overvalued, but trading at a valuation commensurate with a company that is transitioning towards more durable, revenue streams of better mixes. The multiples implied in the valuation represent what appears to be a plausible path of mid- to high-single-digit organic growth and gradual improvement in operating margins, but do not leave allowance for a large valuation gap.

DCF

My updated DCF model using the fiscal 25 exit rate for improved momentum in Industrial and with ~9% WACC and long-term growth of 3.5% puts the intrinsic value of the stock at the mid $220s per share. To me, it seems that at the current price, the stock is fairly valued.

Risks

While demand for AI data centers has been extremely strong, hyperscaler capital expenditures have tended to consolidate in fits and spurts. Therefore, if the acceleration in accelerated compute slows down, or cycles in qualification change, DDN may have some uneven performance on a quarterly basis. Energy appears to be possibly more stable than other areas of the portfolio; however, utility capital expenditures are not immune to the regulatory bottlenecks and rate cycle dynamics. As discussed above, Transportation will continue to be affected by the global trends in automobile production, and sensors look to be structurally challenged in the near term.

Conclusion

The mix change to interconnect and grid infrastructure is now having a material impact on the fundamental characteristics of the company, and Transportation Solutions continues to provide a stable base for the company. While margins are firming and cash generation is continuing to be strong, the portfolio continues to trend away toward a higher percentage of better, structurally sound businesses. However, in view of the appreciation in the price of the stock since my last update, much of this good development is now incorporated in the valuation.
